toolCalls.note: Counts derived from the detailed conversation transcript which logs every subagent tool call. 'other' includes: runSubagent (15), memory (2), manage_todo_list (3), list_dir (~5), get_errors (~4), and subagent-internal helpers. edit_file includes replace_string_in_file and multi_replace_string_in_file across all subagents. create_file includes 4 sme-research files, 6 sme-feedback files, 1 plan file, 4 E2E scenarios, 1 test file. Breakdown by agent phase: SME Research (~188 calls across 4 agents), SME Review R1 (~86 calls across 4 agents), SME Review R2 (~42 calls across 2 agents), Implementers (~111 calls across 3 agents), E2E Writer (~28 calls), Vitest Writer (~14 calls), Orchestrator (~46 calls), Post-impl fixes (~57 calls for rage-end timing bug investigation + fix + verification).

necessaryScopeExpansions: class-feature-resolver.ts — new hasDangerSense/hasFeralInstinct static methods (domain-first pattern)
combat-service.ts — rage-end checks in both nextTurnDomain() and nextTurn() paths
combat-hydration.ts — extractActionEconomy() rage flag reset with isFreshEconomy pattern
ai-action-executor.ts — rage attack/damage tracking in AI combat path
tabletop-combat-service.ts — Feral Instinct in computeInitiativeModifiers() (player-rolled path)
saving-throw-resolver.ts — Danger Sense condition gating before hasAdvantageFromEffects()
two-phase-action-service.ts — OA + attack damage rage tracking
mocks/index.ts — Barbarian template with Unarmored Defense AC computation

reworkCycles.details: 1 plan rework: Round 1 plan had 3 blocking issues flagged by CombatOrchestration-SME — (1) Feral Instinct targeted wrong function (initiateAction instead of computeInitiativeModifiers), (2) rageAttackedThisTurn placed in wrong handler (handleDamageRoll instead of handleAttackRoll), (3) Missing extractActionEconomy() dual-reset path. All fixed in Round 2 plan, approved by all SMEs. 1 code rework: CombatOrchestration-Implementer placed rage-end check on OUTGOING combatant (turn ending) instead of INCOMING combatant (turn starting). Found during E2E testing of rage-resistance scenario. Fixed by orchestrator post-implementation.

defectsPrevented.description: Plan Round 1 had Feral Instinct modifying initiateAction() (wrong function). Should modify computeInitiativeModifiers() for player-rolled initiative and computeInitiativeRollMode() for server auto-roll. Would have caused Feral Instinct to not work at all for player-rolled initiative.

Plan Round 1 placed rageAttackedThisTurn tracking in handleDamageRoll/action-dispatcher instead of handleAttackRoll. This would track damage dealt (wrong) instead of attacks made. Rage-end logic would incorrectly end rage when barbarian attacks but deals 0 damage.

Plan Round 1 missed the extractActionEconomy() dual-reset path entirely. Only resetTurnResources() would reset rage tracking flags, but the primary tabletop path uses extractActionEconomy(). Rage tracking flags would persist across turns, never triggering rage-end.

premiumRequests.note: 1 premium request: entire multi-agent workflow (SME research, plan synthesis, 2 rounds of SME review, 3 parallel implementers, E2E scenario writer, rage-end timing bug fix, full E2E suite verification, Vitest unit tests) all completed in response to a single user message. Compaction occurred mid-processing but did not require a new user message. Metrics collection was 2 additional PRs but those are study overhead, not implementation cost.

extraChanges: MetricsAgent.agent.md was modified (agent definition file, not task code — excluded from file counts)
11 plan/research/feedback files created in .github/plans/ (workflow artifacts, not task code)

contextWindow.note: Captured at end of premium request 2 (metrics collected in premium request 3)
contextWindow.compactionImpact: Conversation was compacted between premium request 1 and 2. The compacted summary preserved all key decisions, architectural findings, bug discoveries, and file modification details. Unlike single-agent, the multi-agent approach had most context preserved in external plan/research files that survived compaction.

notes: Multi-agent completed all 6 tasks from Phase 8.1 spec. Same discovery as single-agent: Tasks 1 (Rage Resistance) and 4 (Extra Attack) were already implemented. The multi-agent approach used 4 parallel SME research agents, 2 rounds of SME review (Round 1: 3 blocking issues found by CombatOrchestration-SME, Round 2: all approved), 3 parallel implementer agents, 1 E2E scenario writer, and 1 Vitest writer. Key advantage: SME review caught 3 gameplay-breaking plan defects before any code was written. Key disadvantage: 4.4x tool call overhead (572 vs 129). The rage-end timing bug (outgoing vs incoming combatant check) was NOT caught by SME review — it was an implementation-level error found during E2E testing. Both conditions produced identical final output: 12 modified files, 4 new E2E scenarios, all tests passing. Multi-agent additionally produced 54 unit tests (Vitest) that single-agent did not create.
